full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I had a professor in seminary who liked to say that a priest should never wear a pectoral cross,<|7.18|> <|7.48|> this large cross worn around the neck, lest your bishop think you want her job.<|13.72|> <|14.38|> So I don't wear my Jerusalem cross much anymore, but it is a prized possession.<|19.88|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When I first joined my church in Pennsylvania, there was a member I kept hearing about who was in Florida for the winter.<|8.10|> <|9.24|> It was clear from the way that folks spoke about Paul that he had a long history with the church,<|15.96|> <|16.48|> had served it well over the years, and was considered by many to be a pillar of the community,<|22.70|> <|23.16|> both within and outside the walls of the church.<|26.54|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.96|> You could tell that people were used to deferring to him in most situations.<|6.20|> <|7.18|> He was powerful, but he was also a wise and faithful man.<|12.30|> <|13.30|> Over the next two years, I had a passing knowledge of him and he of me.<|19.24|> <|20.08|> And other than the times he fussed at my children for getting in the potluck line too early,<|25.98|> <|26.26|> we didn't encounter each other much.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> When I was selected to be senior warden, our paths started to cross more and more.<|7.32|> <|8.04|> And it's safe to say he wasn't always happy about it. I was younger, and I was a woman,<|14.90|> <|15.48|> and I did not always follow his advice. Over time, his frustrations with me turned to<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> respect. I could even get that curmudgeonly octogenarian to laugh, even occasionally at himself.<|8.98|> <|9.94|> We developed a deep respect for each other, and he became one of my biggest advocates when I started to discern my call.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to holy orders. When I was finally made a postulant for ordination, Paul and his wife,<|7.08|> <|7.28|> Jean, gave me this cross as a gift. It was brought back as a souvenir from the Holy Land<|14.10|> <|14.10|> by Jean's mother in the 1920s and had been in their family until they gave it to me.<|20.28|> <|21.48|> I cherish it as a gift, but I'm not sure. I'm not sure. I'm not sure. I'm not sure. I'm not sure.<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> from precious friends.<|2.24|> <|2.24|> But it also symbolizes for me the miraculous work<|6.60|> <|6.60|> the Spirit can do in our lives to bring the unlikeliest<|12.34|> <|12.34|> of people together in a relationship of love and respect.<|16.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.28|> I also find great meaning for my life as a priest in the symbolism of the cross itself.<|7.34|> <|8.22|> The four small crosses that surround the large central cross<|13.52|> <|13.52|> signify the spread of the gospel to all four corners of the earth.<|19.04|> <|19.04|> It is a gift I cherish as I remember both the sacrifice of my life<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Lord and Savior, and as I remember the trust and support of unlikely friends.<|8.44|> <|8.44|> As I remember both suffering and hope.<|14.30|> <|14.30|> The cross you now hold, or upon which you are gazing, what is its story?<|21.56|> <|21.56|> In addition to its meaning for us as Christians,<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How is it important to you?<|2.76|> <|2.76|> How does it connect you to your faith and your community?<|7.60|> <|7.60|> Is it a gift from someone dear to you?<|10.02|> <|10.02|> A reminder of a particular time or place?<|15.40|> <|15.40|> Does it bring you comfort or stir up deep emotion?<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> in you. We invite you on this Good Friday to sit quietly, to venerate the cross that is meaningful<|12.14|> <|12.14|> to you. What does it raise up in you? What might you need to lay at the base of that cross,<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What pain? What unforgiveness? What memory? Or what joy?<|8.08|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I had a professor in seminary who liked to say that a priest should never wear a pectoral cross,<|7.18|>
<|7.48|> this large cross worn around the neck, lest your bishop think you want her job.<|13.72|>
<|14.38|> So I don't wear my Jerusalem cross much anymore, but it is a prized possession.<|19.88|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When I first joined my church in Pennsylvania, there was a member I kept hearing about who was in Florida for the winter.<|8.10|>
<|9.24|> It was clear from the way that folks spoke about Paul that he had a long history with the church,<|15.96|>
<|16.48|> had served it well over the years, and was considered by many to be a pillar of the community,<|22.70|>
<|23.16|> both within and outside the walls of the church.<|26.54|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.96|> You could tell that people were used to deferring to him in most situations.<|6.20|>
<|7.18|> He was powerful, but he was also a wise and faithful man.<|12.30|>
<|13.30|> Over the next two years, I had a passing knowledge of him and he of me.<|19.24|>
<|20.08|> And other than the times he fussed at my children for getting in the potluck line too early,<|25.98|>
<|26.26|> we didn't encounter each other much.<|28.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> When I was selected to be senior warden, our paths started to cross more and more.<|7.32|>
<|8.04|> And it's safe to say he wasn't always happy about it. I was younger, and I was a woman,<|14.90|>
<|15.48|> and I did not always follow his advice. Over time, his frustrations with me turned to<|24.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> respect. I could even get that curmudgeonly octogenarian to laugh, even occasionally at himself.<|8.98|>
<|9.94|> We developed a deep respect for each other, and he became one of my biggest advocates when I started to discern my call.<|18.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to holy orders. When I was finally made a postulant for ordination, Paul and his wife,<|7.08|>
<|7.28|> Jean, gave me this cross as a gift. It was brought back as a souvenir from the Holy Land<|14.10|>
<|14.10|> by Jean's mother in the 1920s and had been in their family until they gave it to me.<|20.28|>
<|21.48|> I cherish it as a gift, but I'm not sure. I'm not sure. I'm not sure. I'm not sure. I'm not sure.<|24.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> from precious friends.<|2.24|>
<|2.24|> But it also symbolizes for me the miraculous work<|6.60|>
<|6.60|> the Spirit can do in our lives to bring the unlikeliest<|12.34|>
<|12.34|> of people together in a relationship of love and respect.<|16.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.28|> I also find great meaning for my life as a priest in the symbolism of the cross itself.<|7.34|>
<|8.22|> The four small crosses that surround the large central cross<|13.52|>
<|13.52|> signify the spread of the gospel to all four corners of the earth.<|19.04|>
<|19.04|> It is a gift I cherish as I remember both the sacrifice of my life<|26.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Lord and Savior, and as I remember the trust and support of unlikely friends.<|8.44|>
<|8.44|> As I remember both suffering and hope.<|14.30|>
<|14.30|> The cross you now hold, or upon which you are gazing, what is its story?<|21.56|>
<|21.56|> In addition to its meaning for us as Christians,<|25.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How is it important to you?<|2.76|>
